n.override.Values,\n1524: \t\t\tRange: n.override.ValuesRange,\n1525: \t\t}, schema)\n1526: \t\tresp = providers.ReadDataSourceResponse{\n1527: \t\t\tState:       override,\n1528: \t\t\tDiagnostics: overrideDiags,\n1529: \t\t}\n1530: \t} else {\n1531: \t\tresp = provider.ReadDataSource(providers.ReadDataSourceRequest{\n1532: \t\t\tTypeName:     n.Addr.ContainingResource().Resource.Type,\n1533: \t\t\tConfig:       configVal,\n1534: \t\t\tProviderMeta: metaConfigVal,\n1535: \t\t\tClientCapabilities: providers.ClientCapabilities{\n1536: \t\t\t\tDeferralAllowed: deferralAllowed,\n1537: \t\t\t},\n1538: \t\t})\n1539: \n1540: \t\t// If we don't support deferrals, but the provider reports a deferral and does not\n1541: \t\t// emit any error level diagnostics, we should emit an error.\n1542: \t\tif resp.Deferred != nil && !deferralAllowed && !resp.Diagnostics.HasErrors() {\n1543: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(deferring.UnexpectedProviderDeferralDiagnostic(n.Addr))\n1544: \t\t}\n1545: \n1546: \t\tif resp.Deferred != nil {\n1547: \t\t\tdeferred = resp.Deferred\n1548: \t\t}\n1549: \t}\n1550: \tdiags = diags.Append(resp.Diagnostics.InConfigBody(config.Config, n.Addr.String()))\n1551: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n1552: \t\treturn newVal, deferred, diags\n1553: \t}\n1554: \tnewVal = resp.State\n1555: \tif newVal == cty.NilVal {\n1556: \t\t// This can happen with incompletely-configured mocks. We'll allow it\n1557: \t\t// and treat it as an alias for a properly-typed null value.\n1558: \t\tnewVal = cty.NullVal(schema.ImpliedType())\n1559: \t}\n1560: \n1561: \t// We don't want to run the checks if the data source read is deferred\n1562: \tif deferred == nil {\n1563: \t\tfor _, err := range newVal.Type().TestConformance(schema.ImpliedType()) {\n1564: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n1565: \t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n1566: \t\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid object\",\n1567: \t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n1568: \t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q produced an invalid value for %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n1569: \t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider, tfdiags.FormatErrorPrefixed(err, n.Addr.String()),\n1570: \t\t\t\t),\n1571: \t\t\t))\n1572: \t\t}\n1573: \t\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n1574: \t\t\treturn newVal, deferred, diags\n1575: \t\t}\n1576: \n1577: \t\tif newVal.IsNull() {\n1578: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n1579: \t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n1580: \t\t\t\t\"Provider produced null object\",\n1581: \t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n1582: \t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q produced a null value for %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n1583: \t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider, n.Addr,\n1584: \t\t\t\t),\n1585: \t\t\t))\n1586: \t\t}\n1587: \n1588: \t\tif !newVal.IsNull() && !newVal.IsWhollyKnown() {\n1589: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n1590: \t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n1591: \t\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid object\",\n1592: \t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n1593: \t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q produced a value for %s that is not wholly known.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n1594: \t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider, n.Addr,\n1595: \t\t\t\t),\n1596: \t\t\t))\n1597: \n1598: \t\t\t// We'll still save the object, but we need to eliminate any unknown\n1599: \t\t\t// values first because we can't serialize them in the state file.\n1600: \t\t\t// Note that this may cause set elements to be coalesced if they\n1601: \t\t\t// differed only by having unknown values, but we don't worry about\n1602: \t\t\t// that here because we're saving the value only for inspection\n1603: \t\t\t// purposes; the error we added above will halt the graph walk.\n1604: \t\t\tnewVal = cty.UnknownAsNull(newVal)\n1605: \t\t}\n1606: \t}\n1607: \tnewVal = newVal.MarkWithPaths(pvm)\n1608: \tif sensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(newVal, nil); len(sensitivePaths) != 0 {\n1609: \t\tnewVal = marks.MarkPaths(newVal, marks.Sensitive, sensitivePaths)\n1610: \t}\n1611: \n1612: \tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n1613: \t\treturn h.PostApply(n.HookResourceIdentity(), addrs.NotDeposed, newVal, diags.Err())\n1614: \t}))\n1615: \n1616: \treturn newVal, deferred, diags\n1617: }\n1618: \n1619: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) providerMetas(ctx EvalContext) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n1620: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n1621: \tmetaConfigVal := cty.NullVal(cty.DynamicPseudoType)\n1622: \n1623: \t_, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\n1624: \tif err != nil {\n1625: \t\treturn metaConfigVal, diags.Append(err)\n1626: \t}\n1627: \tif n.ProviderMetas != nil {\n1628: \t\tif m, ok := n.ProviderMetas[n.ResolvedProvider.Provider]; ok && m != nil {\n1629: \t\t\t// if the provider doesn't support this feature, throw an error\n1630: \t\t\tif providerSchema.ProviderMeta.Block == nil {\n1631: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{\n1632: \t\t\t\t\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\n1633: \t\t\t\t\tSummary:  fmt.Sprintf(\"Provider %s doesn't support provider_meta\", n.ResolvedProvider.Provider.String()),\n1634: \t\t\t\t\tDetail:   fmt.Sprintf(\"The resource %s belongs to a provider that doesn't support provider_meta blocks\", n.Addr.Resource),\n1635: \t\t\t\t\tSubject:  &m.ProviderRange,\n1636: \t\t\t\t})\n1637: \t\t\t} else {\n1638: \t\t\t\tvar configDiags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n1639: \t\t\t\tmetaConfigVal, _, configDiags = ctx.EvaluateBlock(m.Config, providerSchema.ProviderMeta.Block, nil, EvalDataForNoInstanceKey)\n1640: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n1641: \t\t\t}\n1642: \t\t}\n1643: \t}\n1644: \treturn metaConfigVal, diags\n1645: }\n1646: \n1647: // planDataSource deals with the main part of the data resource lifecycle:\n1648: // either actually reading from the data source or generating a plan to do so.\n1649: //\n1650: // currentState is the current state for the data source, and the new state is\n1651: // returned. While data sources are read-only, we need to start with the prior\n1652: // state to determine if we have a change or not.  If we needed to read a new\n1653: // value, but it still matches the previous state, then we can record a NoNop\n1654: // change. If the states don't match then we record a Read change so that the\n1655: // new value is applied to the state.\n1656: //\n1657: // The cases where a data source will generate a planned change instead\n1658: // of finishing during the plan are:\n1659: //\n1660: //   - Its config has unknown values or it depends on a resource with pending changes.\n1661: //     (Note that every data source that is DeferredPrereq should also fit this description.)\n1662: //   - We attempted a read request, but the provider says we're deferred.\n1663: //   - It's nested in a check block, and should always read again during apply.\n1664: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) planDataSource(ctx EvalContext, checkRuleSeverity tfdiags.Severity, skipPlanChanges, dependencyDeferred bool) (*plans.ResourceInstanceChange, *states.ResourceInstanceObject, *providers.Deferred, instances.RepetitionData, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n1665: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n1666: \tvar keyData instances.RepetitionData\n1667: \tvar configVal cty.Value\n1668: \n1669: \tvar deferred *providers.Deferred\n1670: \tif dependencyDeferred {\n1671: \t\t// If a dependency of this data source was deferred, then we're going\n1672: \t\t// to end up deferring this whatever happens. So, our default status\n1673: \t\t// is deferred. If the provider indicates this resource should be\n1674: \t\t// deferred for another reason, that reason should take priority over\n1675: \t\t// this one.\n1676: \t\tdeferred = &providers.Deferred{\n1677: \t\t\tReason: providers.DeferredReasonDeferredPrereq,\n1678: \t\t}\n1679: \t}\n1680: \n1681: \t_, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\n1682: \tif err != nil {\n1683: \t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags.Append(err)\n1684: \t}\n1685: \n1686: \tconfig := *n.Config\n1687: \tschema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(n.Addr.ContainingResource().Resource)\n1688: \tif schema == nil {\n1689: \t\t// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here\n1690: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(fmt.Errorf(\"provider %q does not support data source %q\", n.ResolvedProvider, n.Addr.ContainingResource().Resource.Type))\n1691: \t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n1692: \t}\n1693: \n1694: \tobjTy := schema.ImpliedType()\n1695: \tpriorVal := cty.NullVal(objTy)\n1696: \n1697: \tforEach, _, _ := evaluateForEachExpression(config.ForEach, ctx, false)\n1698: \tkeyData = EvalDataForInstanceKey(n.ResourceInstanceAddr().Resource.Key, forEach)\n1699: \n1700: \tcheckDiags := evalCheckRules(\n1701: \t\taddrs.ResourcePrecondition,\n1702: \t\tn.Config.Preconditions,\n1703: \t\tctx, n.Addr, keyData,\n1704: \t\tcheckRuleSeverity,\n1705: \t)\n1706: \tdiags = diags.Append(checkDiags)\n1707: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n1708: \t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags // failed preconditions prevent further evaluation\n1709: \t}\n1710: \n1711: \tvar configDiags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n1712: \tconfigVal, _, configDiags = ctx.EvaluateBlock(config.Config, schema, nil, keyData)\n1713: \tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n1714: \tdiags = diags.Append(\n1715: \t\tvalidateResourceForbiddenEphemeralValues(ctx, configVal, schema).InConfigBody(n.Config.Config, n.Addr.String()),\n1716: \t)\n1717: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n1718: \t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n1719: \t}\n1720: \tunmarkedConfigVal, unmarkedPaths := configVal.UnmarkDeepWithPaths()\n1721: \n1722: \tcheck, nested := n.nestedInCheckBlock()\n1723: \tif nested {\n1724: \t\t// Going forward from this point, the only reason we will fail is\n1725: \t\t// that the data source fails to load its data. Normally, this would\n1726: \t\t// cancel the entire plan and this error message would bubble its way\n1727: \t\t// back up to the user.\n1728: \t\t//\n1729: \t\t// But, if we are in a check block then we don't want this data block to\n1730: \t\t// cause the plan to fail. We also need to report a status on the data\n1731: \t\t// block so the check processing later on knows whether to attempt to\n1732: \t\t// process the checks. Either we'll report the data block as failed\n1733: \t\t// if/when we load the data block later, or we want to report it as a\n1734: \t\t// success overall.\n1735: \t\t//\n1736: \t\t// Therefore, we create a deferred function here that will check if the\n1737: \t\t// status for the check has been updated yet, and if not we will set it\n1738: \t\t// to be StatusPass. The rest of this function will only update the\n1739: \t\t// status if it should be StatusFail.\n1740: \t\tdefer func() {\n1741: \t\t\tstatus := ctx.Checks().ObjectCheckStatus(check.Addr().Absolute(n.Addr.Module))\n1742: \t\t\tif status == checks.StatusUnknown {\n1743: \t\t\t\tctx.Checks().ReportCheckResult(check.Addr().Absolute(n.Addr.Module), addrs.CheckDataResource, 0, checks.StatusPass)\n1744: \t\t\t}\n1745: \t\t}()\n1746: \t}\n1747: \n1748: \tconfigKnown := configVal.IsWhollyKnown()\n1749: \tdepsPending := n.dependenciesHavePendingChanges(ctx)\n1750: \t// If our configuration contains any unknown values, or we depend on any\n1751: \t// unknown values then we must defer the read to the apply phase by\n1752: \t// producing a \"Read\" change for this resource, and a placeholder value for\n1753: \t// it in the state.\n1754: \tif depsPending || !configKnown {\n1755: \t\t// We can't plan any changes if we're only refreshing, so the only\n1756: \t\t// value we can set here is whatever was in state previously.\n1757: \t\tif skipPlanChanges {\n1758: \t\t\tplannedNewState := &states.ResourceInstanceObject{\n1759: \t\t\t\tValue:  priorVal,\n1760: \t\t\t\tStatus: states.ObjectReady,\n1761: \t\t\t}\n1762: \n1763: \t\t\treturn nil, plannedNewState, deferred, keyData, diags\n1764: \t\t}\n1765: \n1766: \t\tvar reason plans.ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason\n1767: \t\tswitch {\n1768: \t\tcase !configKnown:\n1769: \t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] planDataSource: %s configuration not fully known yet, so deferring to apply phase\", n.Addr)\n1770: \t\t\treason = plans.ResourceInstanceReadBecauseConfigUnknown\n1771: \t\tcase depsPending:\n1772: \t\t\t// NOTE: depsPending can be true at the same time as configKnown\n1773: \t\t\t// is false; configKnown takes precedence because it's more\n1774: \t\t\t// specific.\n1775: \t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] planDataSource: %s configuration is fully known, at least one dependency has changes pending\", n.Addr)\n1776: \t\t\treason = plans.ResourceInstanceReadBecauseDependencyPending\n1777: \t\t}\n1778: \n1779: \t\tproposedNewVal := objchange.PlannedDataResourceObject(schema, unmarkedConfigVal)\n1780: \n1781: \t\t// even though we are only returning the config value because we can't\n1782: \t\t// yet read the data source, we need to incorporate the schema marks so\n1783: \t\t// that downstream consumers can detect them when planning.\n1784: \t\tproposedNewVal = proposedNewVal.MarkWithPaths(unmarkedPaths)\n1785: \t\tif sensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(proposedNewVal, nil); len(sensitivePaths) != 0 {\n1786: \t\t\tproposedNewVal = marks.MarkPaths(proposedNewVal, marks.Sensitive, sensitivePaths)\n1787: \t\t}\n1788: \n1789: \t\t// Apply detects that the data source will need to be read by the After\n1790: \t\t// value containing unknowns from PlanDataResourceObject.\n1791: \t\tplannedChange := &plans.ResourceInstanceChange{\n1792: \t\t\tAddr:         n.Addr,\n1793: \t\t\tPrevRunAddr:  n.prevRunAddr(ctx),\n1794: \t\t\tProviderAddr: n.ResolvedProvider,\n1795: \t\t\tChange: plans.Change{\n1796: \t\t\t\tAction: plans.Read,\n1797: \t\t\t\tBefore: priorVal,\n1798: \t\t\t\tAfter:  proposedNewVal,\n1799: \t\t\t},\n1800: \t\t\tActionReason: reason,\n1801: \t\t}\n1802: \n1803: \t\tplannedNewState := &states.ResourceInstanceObject{\n1804: \t\t\tValue:  proposedNewVal,\n1805: \t\t\tStatus: states.ObjectPlanned,\n1806: \t\t}\n1807: \n1808: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n1809: \t\t\treturn h.PostDiff(n.HookResourceIdentity(), addrs.NotDeposed, plans.Read, priorVal, proposedNewVal)\n1810: \t\t}))\n1811: \n1812: \t\treturn plannedChange, plannedNewState, deferred, keyData, diags\n1813: \t}\n1814: \n1815: \t// We have a complete configuration with no dependencies to wait on, so we\n1816: \t// can read the data source into the state.\n1817: \t// newVal is fully marked by the readDataSource method.\n1818: \tnewVal, readDeferred, readDiags := n.readDataSource(ctx, configVal)\n1819: \n1820: \tif readDeferred != nil {\n1821: \t\t// This will either be null or a value that indicates we're deferred\n1822: \t\t// because of a dependency. In both cases we're happy to just overwrite\n1823: \t\t// that with the more relevant information directly from the provider.\n1824: \t\tdeferred = readDeferred\n1825: \t}\n1826: \n1827: \t// Now we've loaded the data, and diags tells us whether we were successful\n1828: \t// or not, we are going to create our plannedChange and our\n1829: \t// proposedNewState.\n1830: \tvar plannedChange *plans.ResourceInstanceChange\n1831: \tvar plannedNewState *states.ResourceInstanceObject\n1832: \n1833: \t// If we are a nested block, then we want to create a plannedChange that\n1834: \t// tells Terraform to reload the data block during the apply stage even if\n1835: \t// we managed to get the data now.\n1836: \t// Another consideration is that if we failed to load the data, we need to\n1837: \t// disguise that for a nested block. Nested blocks will report the overall\n1838: \t// check as failed but won't affect the rest of the plan operation or block\n1839: \t// an apply operation.\n1840: \n1841: \tif nested {\n1842: \t\taddr := check.Addr().Absolute(n.Addr.Module)\n1843: \n1844: \t\t// Let's fix things up for a nested data block.\n1845: \t\t//\n1846: \t\t// A nested data block doesn't error, and creates a planned change. So,\n1847: \t\t// if we encountered an error we'll tidy up newVal so it makes sense\n1848: \t\t// and handle the error. We'll also create the plannedChange if\n1849: \t\t// appropriate.\n1850: \n1851: \t\tif readDiags.HasErrors() {\n1852: \t\t\t// If we had errors, then we can cover that up by marking the new\n1853: \t\t\t// state as unknown.\n1854: \t\t\tnewVal = objchange.PlannedDataResourceObject(schema, unmarkedConfigVal)\n1855: \n1856: \t\t\t// not only do we want to ensure this synthetic value has the marks,\n1857: \t\t\t// but since this is the value being returned from the data source\n1858: \t\t\t// we need to ensure the schema marks are added as well.\n1859: \t\t\tnewVal = newVal.MarkWithPaths(unmarkedPaths)\n1860: \t\t\tif sensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(newVal, nil); len(sensitivePaths) != 0 {\n1861: \t\t\t\tnewVal = marks.MarkPaths(newVal, marks.Sensitive, sensitivePaths)\n1862: \t\t\t}\n1863: \n1864: \t\t\t// We still want to report the check as failed even if we are still\n1865: \t\t\t// letting it run again during the apply stage.\n1866: \t\t\tctx.Checks().ReportCheckFailure(addr, addrs.CheckDataResource, 0, readDiags.Err().Error())\n1867: \t\t}\n1868: \n1869: \t\t// Any warning or error diagnostics we'll wrap with some special checks\n1870: \t\t// diagnostics. This is so we can identify them later, and so they'll\n1871: \t\t// only report as warnings.\n1872: \t\treadDiags = tfdiags.OverrideAll(readDiags, tfdiags.Warning, func() tfdiags.DiagnosticExtraWrapper {\n1873: \t\t\treturn &addrs.CheckRuleDiagnosticExtra{\n1874: \t\t\t\tCheckRule: addrs.NewCheckRule(addr, addrs.CheckDataResource, 0),\n1875: \t\t\t}\n1876: \t\t})\n1877: \n1878: \t\t// refreshOnly plans cannot produce planned changes, so we only do\n1879: \t\t// this if skipPlanChanges is false. Conversely, provider-deferred data\n1880: \t\t// sources always generate a planned change with a different ActionReason.\n1881: \t\tif !skipPlanChanges && deferred == nil {\n1882: \t\t\tplannedChange = &plans.ResourceInstanceChange{\n1883: \t\t\t\tAddr:         n.Addr,\n1884: \t\t\t\tPrevRunAddr:  n.prevRunAddr(ctx),\n1885: \t\t\t\tProviderAddr: n.ResolvedProvider,\n1886: \t\t\t\tChange: plans.Change{\n1887: \t\t\t\t\tAction: plans.Read,\n1888: \t\t\t\t\tBefore: priorVal,\n1889: \t\t\t\t\tAfter:  newVal,\n1890: \t\t\t\t},\n1891: \t\t\t\tActionReason: plans.ResourceInstanceReadBecauseCheckNested,\n1892: \t\t\t}\n1893: \t\t}\n1894: \t}\n1895: \n1896: \t// Provider-deferred data sources always generate a planned change.\n1897: \tif deferred != nil {\n1898: \t\tplannedChange = &plans.ResourceInstanceChange{\n1899: \t\t\tAddr:         n.Addr,\n1900: \t\t\tPrevRunAddr:  n.prevRunAddr(ctx),\n1901: \t\t\tProviderAddr: n.ResolvedProvider,\n1902: \t\t\tChange: plans.Change{\n1903: \t\t\t\tAction: plans.Read,\n1904: \t\t\t\tBefore: priorVal,\n1905: \t\t\t\tAfter:  newVal,\n1906: \t\t\t},\n1907: \t\t\t// The caller should be more interested in the deferral reason, but this\n1908: \t\t\t// action reason is a reasonable description of what's happening.\n1909: \t\t\tActionReason: plans.ResourceInstanceReadBecauseDependencyPending,\n1910: \t\t}\n1911: \n1912: \t\tplannedNewState = &states.ResourceInstanceObject{\n1913: \t\t\tValue:  newVal,\n1914: \t\t\tStatus: states.ObjectPlanned,\n1915: \t\t}\n1916: \t}\n1917: \n1918: \tdiags = diags.Append(readDiags)\n1919: \tif !diags.HasErrors() && deferred == nil {\n1920: \t\t// Finally, let's make our new state.\n1921: \t\tplannedNewState = &states.ResourceInstanceObject{\n1922: \t\t\tValue:  newVal,\n1923: \t\t\tStatus: states.ObjectReady,\n1924: \t\t}\n1925: \t}\n1926: \n1927: \treturn plannedChange, plannedNewState, deferred, keyData, diags\n1928: }\n1929: \n1930: // nestedInCheckBlock determines if this resource is nested in a Check config\n1931: // block. If so, this resource will be loaded during both plan and apply\n1932: // operations to make sure the check is always giving the latest information.\n1933: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) nestedInCheckBlock() (*configs.Check, bool) {\n1934: \tif n.Config.Container != nil {\n1935: \t\tcheck, ok := n.Config.Container.(*configs.Check)\n1936: \t\treturn check, ok\n1937: \t}\n1938: \treturn nil, false\n1939: }\n1940: \n1941: // dependenciesHavePendingChanges determines whether any managed resource the\n1942: // receiver depends on has a change pending in the plan, in which case we'd\n1943: // need to override the usual behavior of immediately reading from the data\n1944: // source where possible, and instead defer the read until the apply step.\n1945: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) dependenciesHavePendingChanges(ctx EvalContext) bool {\n1946: \tnModInst := n.Addr.Module\n1947: \tnMod := nModInst.Module()\n1948: \n1949: \t// Check and see if any depends_on dependencies have\n1950: \t// changes, since they won't show up as changes in the\n1951: \t// configuration.\n1952: \tchanges := ctx.Changes()\n1953: \n1954: \tdepsToUse := n.dependsOn\n1955: \n1956: \tif n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Mode == addrs.DataResourceMode {\n1957: \t\tif n.Config.HasCustomConditions() {\n1958: \t\t\t// For a data resource with custom conditions we need to look at\n1959: \t\t\t// the full set of resource dependencies -- both direct and\n1960: \t\t\t// indirect -- because an upstream update might be what's needed\n1961: \t\t\t// in order to make a condition pass.\n1962: \t\t\tdepsToUse = n.Dependencies\n1963: \t\t}\n1964: \t}\n1965: \n1966: \tfor _, d := range depsToUse {\n1967: \t\tif d.Resource.Mode == addrs.DataResourceMode {\n1968: \t\t\t// Data sources have no external side effects, so they pose a need\n1969: \t\t\t// to delay this read. If they do have a change planned, it must be\n1970: \t\t\t// because of a dependency on a managed resource, in which case\n1971: \t\t\t// we'll also encounter it in this list of dependencies.\n1972: \t\t\tcontinue\n1973: \t\t}\n1974: \n1975: \t\tfor _, change := range changes.GetChangesForConfigResource(d) {\n1976: \t\t\tchangeModInst := change.Addr.Module\n1977: \t\t\tchangeMod := changeModInst.Module()\n1978: \n1979: \t\t\tif changeMod.Equal(nMod) && !changeModInst.Equal(nModInst) {\n1980: \t\t\t\t// Dependencies are tracked by configuration address, which\n1981: \t\t\t\t// means we may have changes from other instances of parent\n1982: \t\t\t\t// modules. The actual reference can only take effect within\n1983: \t\t\t\t// the same module instance, so skip any that aren't an exact\n1984: \t\t\t\t// match\n1985: \t\t\t\tcontinue\n1986: \t\t\t}\n1987: \n1988: \t\t\tif change != nil && change.Action != plans.NoOp {\n1989: \t\t\t\treturn true\n1990: \t\t\t}\n1991: \t\t}\n1992: \t}\n1993: \treturn false\n1994: }\n1995: \n1996: // apply deals with the main part of the data resource lifecycle: either\n1997: // actually reading from the data source or generating a plan to do so.\n1998: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) applyDataSource(ctx EvalContext, planned *plans.ResourceInstanceChange) (*states.ResourceInstanceObject, instances.RepetitionData, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n1999: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2000: \tvar keyData instances.RepetitionData\n2001: \n2002: \t_, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\n2003: \tif err != nil {\n2004: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags.Append(err)\n2005: \t}\n2006: \tif planned != nil && planned.Action != plans.Read && planned.Action != plans.NoOp {\n2007: \t\t// If any other action gets in here then that's always a bug; this\n2008: \t\t// EvalNode only deals with reading.\n2009: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(fmt.Errorf(\n2010: \t\t\t\"invalid action %s for %s: only Read is supported (this is a bug in Terraform; please report it!)\",\n2011: \t\t\tplanned.Action, n.Addr,\n2012: \t\t))\n2013: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2014: \t}\n2015: \n2016: \tconfig := *n.Config\n2017: \tschema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(n.Addr.ContainingResource().Resource)\n2018: \tif schema == nil {\n2019: \t\t// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here\n2020: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(fmt.Errorf(\"provider %q does not support data source %q\", n.ResolvedProvider, n.Addr.ContainingResource().Resource.Type))\n2021: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2022: \t}\n2023: \n2024: \tforEach, _, _ := evaluateForEachExpression(config.ForEach, ctx, false)\n2025: \tkeyData = EvalDataForInstanceKey(n.Addr.Resource.Key, forEach)\n2026: \n2027: \tcheckDiags := evalCheckRules(\n2028: \t\taddrs.ResourcePrecondition,\n2029: \t\tn.Config.Preconditions,\n2030: \t\tctx, n.Addr, keyData,\n2031: \t\ttfdiags.Error,\n2032: \t)\n2033: \tdiags = diags.Append(checkDiags)\n2034: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n2035: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2036: \t\t\treturn h.PostApply(n.HookResourceIdentity(), addrs.NotDeposed, planned.Before, diags.Err())\n2037: \t\t}))\n2038: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags // failed preconditions prevent further evaluation\n2039: \t}\n2040: \n2041: \tif planned.Action == plans.NoOp {\n2042: \t\t// If we didn't actually plan to read this then we have nothing more\n2043: \t\t// to do; we're evaluating this only for incidentals like the\n2044: \t\t// precondition/postcondition checks.\n2045: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2046: \t}\n2047: \n2048: \tconfigVal, _, configDiags := ctx.EvaluateBlock(config.Config, schema, nil, keyData)\n2049: \tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n2050: \tif configDiags.HasErrors() {\n2051: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2052: \t}\n2053: \n2054: \tnewVal, readDeferred, readDiags := n.readDataSource(ctx, configVal)\n2055: \tif check, nested := n.nestedInCheckBlock(); nested {\n2056: \t\taddr := check.Addr().Absolute(n.Addr.Module)\n2057: \n2058: \t\t// We're just going to jump in here and hide away any errors for nested\n2059: \t\t// data blocks.\n2060: \t\tif readDiags.HasErrors() {\n2061: \t\t\tctx.Checks().ReportCheckFailure(addr, addrs.CheckDataResource, 0, readDiags.Err().Error())\n2062: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.OverrideAll(readDiags, tfdiags.Warning, func() tfdiags.DiagnosticExtraWrapper {\n2063: \t\t\t\treturn &addrs.CheckRuleDiagnosticExtra{\n2064: \t\t\t\t\tCheckRule: addrs.NewCheckRule(addr, addrs.CheckDataResource, 0),\n2065: \t\t\t\t}\n2066: \t\t\t}))\n2067: \t\t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2068: \t\t}\n2069: \n2070: \t\t// Even though we know there are no errors here, we still want to\n2071: \t\t// identify these diags has having been generated from a check block.\n2072: \t\treadDiags = tfdiags.OverrideAll(readDiags, tfdiags.Warning, func() tfdiags.DiagnosticExtraWrapper {\n2073: \t\t\treturn &addrs.CheckRuleDiagnosticExtra{\n2074: \t\t\t\tCheckRule: addrs.NewCheckRule(addr, addrs.CheckDataResource, 0),\n2075: \t\t\t}\n2076: \t\t})\n2077: \n2078: \t\t// If no errors, just remember to report this as a success and continue\n2079: \t\t// as normal.\n2080: \t\tctx.Checks().ReportCheckResult(addr, addrs.CheckDataResource, 0, checks.StatusPass)\n2081: \t}\n2082: \n2083: \tdiags = diags.Append(readDiags)\n2084: \tif readDiags.HasErrors() {\n2085: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2086: \t}\n2087: \n2088: \tif readDeferred != nil {\n2089: \t\t// Just skip data sources that are being deferred. Nothing, that\n2090: \t\t// references them should be calling them.\n2091: \t\treturn nil, keyData, diags\n2092: \t}\n2093: \n2094: \tstate := &states.ResourceInstanceObject{\n2095: \t\tValue:  newVal,\n2096: \t\tStatus: states.ObjectReady,\n2097: \t}\n2098: \n2099: \treturn state, keyData, diags\n2100: }\n2101: \n2102: // evalApplyProvisioners determines if provisioners need to be run, and if so\n2103: // executes the provisioners for a resource and returns an updated error if\n2104: // provisioning fails.\n2105: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) evalApplyProvisioners(ctx EvalContext, state *states.ResourceInstanceObject, createNew bool, when configs.ProvisionerWhen) tfdiags.Diagnostics {\n2106: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2107: \n2108: \tif state == nil {\n2109: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalApplyProvisioners: %s has no state, so skipping provisioners\", n.Addr)\n2110: \t\treturn nil\n2111: \t}\n2112: \tif when == configs.ProvisionerWhenCreate && !createNew {\n2113: \t\t// If we're not creating a new resource, then don't run provisioners\n2114: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalApplyProvisioners: %s is not freshly-created, so no provisioning is required\", n.Addr)\n2115: \t\treturn nil\n2116: \t}\n2117: \tif state.Status == states.ObjectTainted {\n2118: \t\t// No point in provisioning an object that is already tainted, since\n2119: \t\t// it's going to get recreated on the next apply anyway.\n2120: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalApplyProvisioners: %s is tainted, so skipping provisioning\", n.Addr)\n2121: \t\treturn nil\n2122: \t}\n2123: \n2124: \tvar allProvs []*configs.Provisioner\n2125: \tswitch {\n2126: \tcase n.Config != nil && n.Config.Managed != nil:\n2127: \t\tallProvs = n.Config.Managed.Provisioners\n2128: \tcase n.RemovedConfig != nil && n.RemovedConfig.Managed != nil:\n2129: \t\tallProvs = n.RemovedConfig.Managed.Provisioners\n2130: \t}\n2131: \n2132: \tprovs := filterProvisioners(allProvs, when)\n2133: \tif len(provs) == 0 {\n2134: \t\t// We have no provisioners, so don't do anything\n2135: \t\treturn nil\n2136: \t}\n2137: \n2138: \t// Call pre hook\n2139: \tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2140: \t\treturn h.PreProvisionInstance(n.HookResourceIdentity(), state.Value)\n2141: \t}))\n2142: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n2143: \t\treturn diags\n2144: \t}\n2145: \n2146: \t// If there are no errors, then we append it to our output error\n2147: \t// if we have one, otherwise we just output it.\n2148: \tdiags = diags.Append(n.applyProvisioners(ctx, state, when, provs))\n2149: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n2150: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] evalApplyProvisioners: %s provisioning failed, but we will continue anyway at the caller's request\", n.Addr)\n2151: \t\treturn diags\n2152: \t}\n2153: \n2154: \t// Call post hook\n2155: \treturn diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2156: \t\treturn h.PostProvisionInstance(n.HookResourceIdentity(), state.Value)\n2157: \t}))\n2158: }\n2159: \n2160: // filterProvisioners filters the provisioners on the resource to only\n2161: // the provisioners specified by the \"when\" option.\n2162: func filterProvisioners(configured []*configs.Provisioner, when configs.ProvisionerWhen) []*configs.Provisioner {\n2163: \tif len(configured) == 0 {\n2164: \t\treturn nil\n2165: \t}\n2166: \n2167: \tresult := make([]*configs.Provisioner, 0, len(configured))\n2168: \tfor _, p := range configured {\n2169: \t\tif p.When == when {\n2170: \t\t\tresult = append(result, p)\n2171: \t\t}\n2172: \t}\n2173: \n2174: \treturn result\n2175: }\n2176: \n2177: // applyProvisioners executes the provisioners for a resource.\n2178: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) applyProvisioners(ctx EvalContext, state *states.ResourceInstanceObject, when configs.ProvisionerWhen, provs []*configs.Provisioner) tfdiags.Diagnostics {\n2179: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2180: \n2181: \t// this self is only used for destroy provisioner evaluation, and must\n2182: \t// refer to the last known value of the resource.\n2183: \tself := state.Value\n2184: \n2185: \tvar evalScope func(EvalContext, hcl.Body, cty.Value, *configschema.Block) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics)\n2186: \tswitch when {\n2187: \tcase configs.ProvisionerWhenDestroy:\n2188: \t\tevalScope = n.evalDestroyProvisionerConfig\n2189: \tdefault:\n2190: \t\tevalScope = n.evalProvisionerConfig\n2191: \t}\n2192: \n2193: \t// If there's a connection block defined directly inside the resource block\n2194: \t// then it'll serve as a base connection configuration for all of the\n2195: \t// provisioners.\n2196: \tvar baseConn hcl.Body\n2197: \tswitch {\n2198: \tcase n.Config != nil && n.Config.Managed != nil && n.Config.Managed.Connection != nil:\n2199: \t\tbaseConn = n.Config.Managed.Connection.Config\n2200: \tcase n.RemovedConfig != nil && n.RemovedConfig.Managed != nil && n.RemovedConfig.Managed.Connection != nil:\n2201: \t\tbaseConn = n.RemovedConfig.Managed.Connection.Config\n2202: \t}\n2203: \n2204: \tfor _, prov := range provs {\n2205: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] applyProvisioners: provisioning %s with %q\", n.Addr, prov.Type)\n2206: \n2207: \t\t// Get the provisioner\n2208: \t\tprovisioner, err := ctx.Provisioner(prov.Type)\n2209: \t\tif err != nil {\n2210: \t\t\treturn diags.Append(err)\n2211: \t\t}\n2212: \n2213: \t\tschema, err := ctx.ProvisionerSchema(prov.Type)\n2214: \t\tif err != nil {\n2215: \t\t\t// This error probably won't be a great diagnostic, but in practice\n2216: \t\t\t// we typically catch this problem long before we get here, so\n2217: \t\t\t// it should be rare to return via this codepath.\n2218: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(err)\n2219: \t\t\treturn diags\n2220: \t\t}\n2221: \n2222: \t\tconfig, configDiags := evalScope(ctx, prov.Config, self, schema)\n2223: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n2224: \t\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n2225: \t\t\treturn diags\n2226: \t\t}\n2227: \n2228: \t\t// If the provisioner block contains a connection block of its own then\n2229: \t\t// it can override the base connection configuration, if any.\n2230: \t\tvar localConn hcl.Body\n2231: \t\tif prov.Connection != nil {\n2232: \t\t\tlocalConn = prov.Connection.Config\n2233: \t\t}\n2234: \n2235: \t\tvar connBody hcl.Body\n2236: \t\tswitch {\n2237: \t\tcase baseConn != nil && localConn != nil:\n2238: \t\t\t// Our standard merging logic applies here, similar to what we do\n2239: \t\t\t// with _override.tf configuration files: arguments from the\n2240: \t\t\t// base connection block will be masked by any arguments of the\n2241: \t\t\t// same name in the local connection block.\n2242: \t\t\tconnBody = configs.MergeBodies(baseConn, localConn)\n2243: \t\tcase baseConn != nil:\n2244: \t\t\tconnBody = baseConn\n2245: \t\tcase localConn != nil:\n2246: \t\t\tconnBody = localConn\n2247: \t\t}\n2248: \n2249: \t\t// start with an empty connInfo\n2250: \t\tconnInfo := cty.NullVal(connectionBlockSupersetSchema.ImpliedType())\n2251: \n2252: \t\tif connBody != nil {\n2253: \t\t\tvar connInfoDiags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2254: \t\t\tconnInfo, connInfoDiags = evalScope(ctx, connBody, self, connectionBlockSupersetSchema)\n2255: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(connInfoDiags)\n2256: \t\t\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n2257: \t\t\t\treturn diags\n2258: \t\t\t}\n2259: \t\t}\n2260: \n2261: \t\t{\n2262: \t\t\t// Call pre hook\n2263: \t\t\terr := ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2264: \t\t\t\treturn h.PreProvisionInstanceStep(n.HookResourceIdentity(), prov.Type)\n2265: \t\t\t})\n2266: \t\t\tif err != nil {\n2267: \t\t\t\treturn diags.Append(err)\n2268: \t\t\t}\n2269: \t\t}\n2270: \n2271: \t\t// The output function\n2272: \t\toutputFn := func(msg string) {\n2273: \t\t\tctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2274: \t\t\t\th.ProvisionOutput(n.HookResourceIdentity(), prov.Type, msg)\n2275: \t\t\t\treturn HookActionContinue, nil\n2276: \t\t\t})\n2277: \t\t}\n2278: \n2279: \t\t// If our config or connection info contains any marked values, ensure\n2280: \t\t// those are stripped out before sending to the provisioner. Unlike\n2281: \t\t// resources, we have no need to capture the marked paths and reapply\n2282: \t\t// later.\n2283: \t\tunmarkedConfig, configMarks := config.UnmarkDeep()\n2284: \t\tunmarkedConnInfo, _ := connInfo.UnmarkDeep()\n2285: \n2286: \t\t// Marks on the config might result in leaking sensitive values through\n2287: \t\t// provisioner logging, so we conservatively suppress all output in\n2288: \t\t// this case. This should not apply to connection info values, which\n2289: \t\t// provisioners ought not to be logging anyway.\n2290: \t\tif len(configMarks) > 0 {\n2291: \t\t\toutputFn = func(msg string) {\n2292: \t\t\t\tctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2293: \t\t\t\t\th.ProvisionOutput(n.HookResourceIdentity(), prov.Type, \"(output suppressed due to sensitive value in config)\")\n2294: \t\t\t\t\treturn HookActionContinue, nil\n2295: \t\t\t\t})\n2296: \t\t\t}\n2297: \t\t}\n2298: \n2299: \t\toutput := CallbackUIOutput{OutputFn: outputFn}\n2300: \t\tresp := provisioner.ProvisionResource(provisioners.ProvisionResourceRequest{\n2301: \t\t\tConfig:     unmarkedConfig,\n2302: \t\t\tConnection: unmarkedConnInfo,\n2303: \t\t\tUIOutput:   &output,\n2304: \t\t})\n2305: \t\tapplyDiags := resp.Diagnostics.InConfigBody(prov.Config, n.Addr.String())\n2306: \n2307: \t\t// Call post hook\n2308: \t\thookErr := ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n2309: \t\t\treturn h.PostProvisionInstanceStep(n.HookResourceIdentity(), prov.Type, applyDiags.Err())\n2310: \t\t})\n2311: \n2312: \t\tswitch prov.OnFailure {\n2313: \t\tcase configs.ProvisionerOnFailureContinue:\n2314: \t\t\tif applyDiags.HasErrors() {\n2315: \t\t\t\tlog.Printf(\"[WARN] Errors while provisioning %s with %q, but continuing as requested in configuration\", n.Addr, prov.Type)\n2316: \t\t\t} else {\n2317: \t\t\t\t// Maybe there are warnings that we still want to see\n2318: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(applyDiags)\n2319: \t\t\t}\n2320: \t\tdefault:\n2321: \t\t\t\treturn diags\n2325: \t\t\t}\n2326: \t\t}\n2327: \n2328: \t\t// Deal with the hook\n2329: \t\tif hookErr != nil {\n2330: \t\t\treturn diags.Append(hookErr)\n2331: \t\t}\n2332: \t}\n2333: \n2334: \treturn diags\n2335: }\n2336: \n2337: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) evalProvisionerConfig(ctx EvalContext, body hcl.Body, self cty.Value, schema *configschema.Block) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n2338: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2339: \n2340: \tforEach, _, forEachDiags := evaluateForEachExpression(n.Config.ForEach, ctx, false)\n2341: \tdiags = diags.Append(forEachDiags)\n2342: \n2343: \tkeyData := EvalDataForInstanceKey(n.ResourceInstanceAddr().Resource.Key, forEach)\n2344: \n2345: \tconfig, _, configDiags := ctx.EvaluateBlock(body, schema, n.ResourceInstanceAddr().Resource, keyData)\n2346: \tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n2347: \n2348: \treturn config, diags\n2349: }\n2350: \n2351: // during destroy a provisioner can only evaluate within the scope of the parent resource\n2352: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) evalDestroyProvisionerConfig(ctx EvalContext, body hcl.Body, self cty.Value, schema *configschema.Block) (cty.Value, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n2353: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2354: \n2355: \t// For a destroy-time provisioner forEach is intentionally nil here,\n2356: \t// which EvalDataForInstanceKey That's okay because each.value is prohibited for\n2358: \t// destroy-time provisioners.\n2359: \tkeyData := EvalDataForInstanceKey(n.ResourceInstanceAddr().Resource.Key, nil)\n2360: \n2361: \tevalScope := ctx.EvaluationScope(n.ResourceInstanceAddr().Resource, nil, keyData)\n2362: \tconfig, evalDiags := evalScope.EvalSelfBlock(body, self, schema, keyData)\n2363: \tdiags = diags.Append(evalDiags)\n2364: \n2365: \treturn config, diags\n2366: }\n2367: \n2368: // apply accepts an applyConfig, instead of using n.Config, so destroy plans can\n2369: // send a nil config. The keyData information can be empty if the config is\n2370: // nil, since it is only used to evaluate the configuration.\n2371: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) apply(\n2372: \tctx EvalContext,\n2373: \tstate *states.ResourceInstanceObject,\n2374: \tchange *plans.ResourceInstanceChange,\n2375: \tapplyConfig *configs.Resource,\n2376: \tkeyData instances.RepetitionData,\n2377: \tcreateBeforeDestroy bool) (*states.ResourceInstanceObject, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n2378: \n2379: \tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2380: \tif state == nil {\n2381: \t\tstate = &states.ResourceInstanceObject{}\n2382: \t}\n2383: \n2384: \tif change.Action == plans.NoOp {\n2385: \t\t// If this is a no-op change then we don't want to actually change\n2386: \t\t// anything, so we'll just echo back the state we were given and\n2387: \t\t// let our internal checks and updates proceed.\n2388: \t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] NodeAbstractResourceInstance.apply: skipping %s because it has no planned action\", n.Addr)\n2389: \t\treturn state, diags\n2390: \t}\n2391: \n2392: \tprovider, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\n2393: \tif err != nil {\n2394: \t\treturn nil, diags.Append(err)\n2395: \t}\n2396: \tschema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceType(n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Mode, n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type)\n2397: \tif schema == nil {\n2398: \t\t// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here\n2399: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(fmt.Errorf(\"provider does not support resource type %q\", n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type))\n2400: \t\treturn nil, diags\n2401: \t}\n2402: \n2403: \tlog.Printf(\"[INFO] Starting apply for %s\", n.Addr)\n2404: \n2405: \tconfigVal := cty.NullVal(cty.DynamicPseudoType)\n2406: \tif applyConfig != nil {\n2407: \t\tvar configDiags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2408: \t\tconfigVal, _, configDiags = ctx.EvaluateBlock(applyConfig.Config, schema, nil, keyData)\n2409: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n2410: \t\tif configDiags.HasErrors() {\n2411: \t\t\treturn nil, diags\n2412: \t\t}\n2413: \t}\n2414: \n2415: \tif !configVal.IsWhollyKnown() {\n2416: \t\t// We don't have a pretty format function for a path, but since this is\n2417: \t\t// such a rare error, we can just drop the raw GoString values in here\n2418: \t\t// to make sure we have something to debug with.\n2419: \t\tvar unknownPaths []string\n2420: \t\tcty.Transform(configVal, func(p cty.Path, v cty.Value) (cty.Value, error) {\n2421: \t\t\tif !v.IsKnown() {\n2422: \t\t\t\tunknownPaths = append(unknownPaths, fmt.Sprintf(\"%#v\", p))\n2423: \t\t\t}\n2424: \t\t\treturn v, nil\n2425: \t\t})\n2426: \n2427: \t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n2428: please report it!)\\n\"+\n2431: \t\t\t\t\"The following paths in the resource configuration are unknown:\\n%s\",\n2432: \t\t\t\tn.Addr,\n2433: \t\t\t\tstrings.Join(unknownPaths, \"\\n\"),\n2434: \t\t\t),\n2435: \t\t))\n2436: \t\treturn nil, diags\n2437: \t}\n2438: \n2439: \tmetaConfigVal, metaDiags := n.providerMetas(ctx)\n2440: \tdiags = diags.Append(metaDiags)\n2441: \tif diags.HasErrors() {\n2442: \t\treturn nil, diags\n2443: \t}\n2444: \n2445: \tlog.Printf(\"[DEBUG] %s: applying the planned %s change\", n.Addr, change.Action)\n2446: \n2447: \t// If our config, Before or After value contain any marked values,\n2448: \t// ensure those are stripped out before sending\n2449: \t// this to the provider\n2450: \tunmarkedConfigVal, _ := configVal.UnmarkDeep()\n2451: \tunmarkedBefore, beforePaths := change.Before.UnmarkDeepWithPaths()\n2452: \tunmarkedAfter, afterPaths := change.After.UnmarkDeepWithPaths()\n2453: \n2454: \t// If we have an Update action, our before and after values are equal,\n2455: \t// and only differ on their sensitivity, the newVal is the after val\n2456: \t// and we should not communicate with the provider. We do need to update\n2457: \t// the state with this new value, to ensure the sensitivity change is\n2458: \t// persisted.\n2459: \teqV := unmarkedBefore.Equals(unmarkedAfter)\n2460: \teq := eqV.IsKnown() && eqV.True()\n2461: \tif change.Action == plans.Update && eq && !marks.MarksEqual(beforePaths, afterPaths) {\n2462: \t\t// Copy the previous state, changing only the value\n2463: \t\tnewState := &states.ResourceInstanceObject{\n2464: \t\t\tCreateBeforeDestroy: state.CreateBeforeDestroy,\n2465: \t\t\tDependencies:        state.Dependencies,\n2466: \t\t\tPrivate:             state.Private,\n2467: \t\t\tStatus:              state.Status,\n2468: \t\t\tValue:               change.After,\n2469: \t\t}\n2470: \t\treturn newState, diags\n2471: \t}\n2472: \n2473: \tvar resp providers.ApplyResourceChangeResponse\n2474: \tif n.override != nil {\n2475: \t\t// As with the planning stage, we only need to worry about computed\n2476: \t\t// values the first time the object is created. Otherwise, we're happy\n2477: \t\t// to just apply whatever the user asked for.\n2478: \t\tif change.Action == plans.Create {\n2479: \t\t\toverride, overrideDiags := mocking.ApplyComputedValuesForResource(unmarkedAfter, mocking.MockedData{\n2480: \t\t\t\tValue: n.override.Values,\n2481: \t\t\t\tRange: n.override.ValuesRange,\n2482: \t\t\t}, schema)\n2483: \t\t\tresp = providers.ApplyResourceChangeResponse{\n2484: \t\t\t\tNewState:    override,\n2485: \t\t\t\tDiagnostics: overrideDiags,\n2486: \t\t\t}\n2487: \t\t} else {\n2488: \t\t\tresp = providers.ApplyResourceChangeResponse{\n2489: \t\t\t\tNewState: unmarkedAfter,\n2490: \t\t\t}\n2491: \t\t}\n2492: \t} else {\n2493: \t\tresp = provider.ApplyResourceChange(providers.ApplyResourceChangeRequest{\n2494: \t\t\tTypeName:       n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type,\n2495: \t\t\tPriorState:     unmarkedBefore,\n2496: \t\t\tConfig:         unmarkedConfigVal,\n2497: \t\t\tPlannedState:   unmarkedAfter,\n2498: \t\t\tPlannedPrivate: change.Private,\n2499: \t\t\tProviderMeta:   metaConfigVal,\n2500: \t\t})\n2501: \t}\n2502: \tapplyDiags := resp.Diagnostics\n2503: \tif applyConfig != nil {\n2504: \t\tapplyDiags = applyDiags.InConfigBody(applyConfig.Config, n.Addr.String())\n2505: \t}\n2506: \tdiags = diags.Append(applyDiags)\n2507: \n2508: \t// Even if there are errors in the returned diagnostics, the provider may\n2509: \t// have returned a _partial_ state for an object that already exists but\n2510: \t// failed to fully configure, and so the remaining code must always run\n2511: \t// to completion but must be defensive against the new value being\n2512: \t// incomplete.\n2513: \tnewVal := resp.NewState\n2514: \n2515: \tif newVal == cty.NilVal {\n2516: \t\t// Providers are supposed to return a partial new value even when errors\n2517: \t\t// occur, but sometimes they don't and so in that case we'll patch that up\n2518: \t\t// by just using the prior state, so we'll at least keep track of the\n2519: \t\t// object for the user to retry.\n2520: \t\tnewVal = change.Before\n2521: \n2522: \t\t// As a special case, we'll set the new value to null if it looks like\n2523: \t\t// we were trying to execute a delete, because the provider in this case\n2524: \t\t// probably left the newVal unset intending it to be interpreted as \"null\".\n2525: \t\tif change.After.IsNull() {\n2526: \t\t\tnewVal = cty.NullVal(schema.ImpliedType())\n2527: \t\t}\n2528: \n2529: \t\tif !diags.HasErrors() {\n2530: \t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n2531: \t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n2532: \t\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid object\",\n2533: \t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n2534: \t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q produced an invalid nil value after apply for %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n2535: \t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.String(), n.Addr.String(),\n2536: \t\t\t\t),\n2537: \t\t\t))\n2538: \t\t}\n2539: \t}\n2540: \n2541: \tvar conformDiags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n2542: \tfor _, err := range newVal.Type().TestConformance(schema.ImpliedType()) {\n2543: \t\tconformDiags = conformDiags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n2544: \t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n2545: \t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid object\",\n2546: \t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n2547: \t\t\t\t\"Provider %q produced an invalid value after apply for %s. The result cannot not be saved in the Terraform state.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n2548: \t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.String(), tfdiags.FormatErrorPrefixed(err, n.Addr.String()),\n2549: \t\t\t),\n2550: \t\t))\n2551: \t}\n2552: \tdiags = diags.Append(conformDiags)\n2553: \tif conformDiags.HasErrors() {\n2554: \t\t// Bail early in this particular case, because an object that doesn't\n2555: \t\t// conform to the schema can't be saved in the state anyway -- the\n2556: \t\t// serializer will reject it.\n2557: \t\treturn nil, diags\n2558: \t}\n2559: \n2560: \t// After this point we have a type-conforming result object and so we\n2561: \t// must always run to completion to ensure it can be saved. If n.Error\n2562: \t// is set then we must not return a non-nil error, in order to allow\n2563: \t// evaluation to continue to a later point where our state object will\n2564: \t// be saved.\n2565: \n2566: \t// By this point there must not be any unknown values remaining in our\n2567: \t// object, because we've applied the change and we can't save unknowns\n2568: \t// in our persistent state. If any are present then we will indicate an\n2569: \t// error (which is always a bug in the provider) but we will also replace\n2570: \t// them with nulls so that we can successfully save the portions of the\n2571: \t// returned value that are known.\n2572: \tif !newVal.IsWhollyKnown() {\n2573: \t\t// To generate better error messages, we'll go for a walk through the\n2574: \t\t// value and make a separate diagnostic for each unknown value we\n2575: \t\t// find.\n2576: \t\tcty.Walk(newVal, func(path cty.Path, val cty.Value) (bool, error) {\n2577: \t\t\tif !val.IsKnown() {\n2578: \t\t\t\tpathStr := tfdiags.FormatCtyPath(path)\n2579: \t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n2580: \t\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n2581: \t\t\t\t\t\"Provider returned invalid result object after apply\",\n2582: \t\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n2583: \t\t\t\t\t\t\"After the apply operation, the provider still indicated an unknown value for %s%s. All values must be known after apply, so this is always a bug in the provider and should be reported in the provider's own repository. Terraform will still save the other known object values in the state.\",\n2584: \t\t\t\t\t\tn.Addr, pathStr,\n2585: \t\t\t\t\t),\n2586: \t\t\t\t))\n2587: \t\t\t}\n2588: \t\t\treturn true, nil\n2589: \t\t})\n2590: \n2591: \t\t// NOTE: This operation can potentially be lossy if there are multiple\n2592: \t\t// elements in a set that differ only by unknown values: after\n2593: \t\t// replacing with null these will be merged together into a single set\n2594: \t\t// element. Since we can only get here in the presence of a provider\n2595: \t\t// bug, we accept this because storing a result here is always a\n2596: \t\t// best-effort sort of thing.\n2597: \t\tnewVal = cty.UnknownAsNull(newVal)\n2598: \t}\n2599: \n2600: \t// If we have paths to mark, mark those on this new value we need to\n2601: \t// re-check the value against the schema, because nested computed values\n2602: \t// won't be included in afterPaths, which are only what was read from the\n2603: \t// After plan value.\n2604: \tnewVal = newVal.MarkWithPaths(afterPaths)\n2605: \tif sensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(newVal, nil); len(sensitivePaths) != 0 {\n2606: \t\tnewVal = marks.MarkPaths(newVal, marks.Sensitive, sensitivePaths)\n2607: \t}\n2608: \n2609: \tif change.Action != plans.Delete && !diags.HasErrors() {\n2610: \t\t// Only values that were marked as unknown in the planned value are allowed\n2611: \t\t// to change during the apply operation. (We do this after the unknown-ness\n2612: \t\t// check above so that we also catch anything that became unknown after\n2613: \t\t// being known during plan.)\n2614: \t\t//\n2615: \t\t// If we are returning other errors anyway then we'll give this\n2616: \t\t// a pass since the other errors are usually the explanation for\n2617: \t\t// this one and so it's more helpful to let the user focus on the\n2618: \t\t// root cause rather than distract with this extra problem.\n2619: \t\tif errs := objchange.AssertObjectCompatible(schema, change.After, newVal); len(errs) > 0 {\n2620: \t\t\tif resp.LegacyTypeSystem {\n2621: \t\t\t\t// The shimming of the old type system in the legacy SDK is not precise\n2622: \t\t\t\t// enough to pass this consistency check, so we'll give it a pass here,\n2623: \t\t\t\t// but we will generate a warning about it so that we are more likely\n2624: \t\t\t\t// to notice in the logs if an inconsistency beyond the type system\n2625: \t\t\t\t// leads to a downstream provider failure.\n2626: \t\t\t\tvar buf strings.Builder\n2627: \t\t\t\tfmt.Fprintf(&buf, \"[WARN] Provider %q produced an unexpected new value for %s, but we are tolerating it because it is using the legacy plugin SDK.\\n    The following problems may be the cause of any confusing errors from downstream operations:\", n.ResolvedProvider.String(), n.Addr)\n2628: \t\t\t\tfor _, err := range errs {\n2629: \t\t\t\t\tfmt.Fprintf(&buf, \"\\n      - %s\", tfdiags.FormatError(err))\n2630: \t\t\t\t}\n2631: \t\t\t\tlog.Print(buf.String())\n2632: \n2633: \t\t\t\t// The sort of inconsistency we won't catch here is if a known value\n2634: \t\t\t\t// in the plan is changed during apply. We must only do this for null\n2687: \t\t// new value though, or else we may discard partial updates the\n2688: \t\t// provider was able to complete. has become degraded in an immutable infrastructure scenario and so\n2741: \t// replacing it with a new object is a viable repair path.\n2742: \tmatchedForceReplace := false\n2743: \tfor _, providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(absAddr.ContainingResource().Resource)\n\tif schema == nil {\n\t\t// It shouldn't be possible to get this far in any real scenario\n\t\t// without a schema, but we absAddr)\n\t}\n\n\tsrc, err := obj.Encode(schema.ImpliedType(), currentVersion)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn fmt.Errorf(\"failed to encode %s in state: %s\", absAddr, err)\n\t}\n\n\twrite(src)\n\treturn nil\n}\n\n// planDestroy returns a plain destroy diff.\nfunc (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) planDestroy(ctx EvalContext, currentState *states.ResourceInstanceObject, deposedKey states.DeposedKey) (*plans.ResourceInstanceChange, *providers.Deferred, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n\tvar deferred *providers.Deferred\n\tvar plan *plans.ResourceInstanceChange\n\n\tabsAddr := n.Addr\n\tdeferralAllowed := ctx.Deferrals().DeferralAllowed()\n\n\tif n.ResolvedProvider.Provider.Type == \"\" {\n\t\tif deposedKey == \"\" {\n\t\t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"planDestroy for %s does not have ProviderAddr set\", absAddr))\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"planDestroy for %s (deposed %s) does not have ProviderAddr set\", absAddr, deposedKey))\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\t// If there is no state or our attributes object is null then we're already\n\t// destroyed.\n\tif currentState == nil || currentState.Value.IsNull() {\n\t\t// We still need to generate a NoOp change, because that allows\n\t\t// outside consumers of the plan to distinguish between us affirming\n\t\t// that we checked something and concluded no changes were needed\n\t\t// vs. that something being entirely excluded e.g. due to -target.\n\t\tnoop := &plans.ResourceInstanceChange{\n\t\t\tAddr:        absAddr,\n\t\t\tPrevRunAddr: n.prevRunAddr(ctx),\n\t\t\tDeposedKey:  deposedKey,\n\t\t\tChange: plans.Change{\n\t\t\t\tAction: plans.NoOp,\n\t\t\t\tBefore: diags.Append(err)\n\t}\n\n\tmetaConfigVal, metaDiags := n.providerMetas(ctx)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(metaDiags)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn plan, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\tvar resp unmarkedPriorVal,\n\t\t\tProposedNewState: nullVal,\n\t\t\tPriorPrivate:     currentState.Private,\n\t\t\tProviderMeta:     metaConfigVal,\n\t\t\tClientCapabilities: providers.ClientCapabilities{\n\t\t\t\tDeferralAllowed: deferralAllowed,\n\t\t\t},\n\t\t})\n\t\tdeferred = resp.Deferred\n\n\t\t// If we don't support deferrals, but the provider reports a deferral and does not\n\t\t// emit any error level diagnostics, we should emit an error.\n\t\tif resp.Deferred != nil && !deferralAllowed && !resp.Diagnostics.HasErrors() {\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(deferring.UnexpectedProviderDeferralDiagnostic(n.Addr))\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// We may not have a config for all destroys, but we want to reference\n\t\t// it in the diagnostics if we do.\n\t\tif n.Config != nil {\n\t\t\tresp.Diagnostics = resp.Diagnostics.InConfigBody(n.Config.Config, n.Addr.String())\n\t\t}\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(resp.Diagnostics)\n\t\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\t\treturn plan, deferred, diags\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// Check that the provider returned a null value here, since that is the\n\t\t// only valid value for a destroy plan.\n\t\tif !resp.PlannedState.IsNull() && deferred == nil {\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid plan\",\n\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n\t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q planned a non-null destroy value for %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue &plans.ResourceInstanceChange{\n\t\tAddr:        absAddr,\n\t\tPrevRunAddr: n.prevRunAddr(ctx),\n\t\tDeposedKey:  deposedKey,\n\t\tChange: plans.Change{\n\t\t\tAction: plans.Delete,\n\t\t\tBefore: of\n// global planned changes.\nfunc (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) writeChange(ctx EvalContext, change *plans.ResourceInstanceChange, deposedKey states.DeposedKey) error {\n\tchanges := ctx.Changes()\n\n\tif change == nil {\n\t\t// Caller sets nil to indicate that we need to remove a change from\n\t\t// the set of changes.\n\t\tchanges.RemoveResourceInstanceChange(n.Addr, deposedKey)\n\t\treturn nil\n\t}\n\n\tif change.Addr.String() != n.Addr.String() || change.DeposedKey != deposedKey {\n\t\t// Should never happen, and indicates a bug in the caller.\n\t\tpanic(\"inconsistent address and/or deposed key in writeChange\")\n\t}\n\tif change.PrevRunAddr.Resource.Resource.Type == \"\" {\n\t\t// Should never happen, and indicates a bug in the caller.\n\t\t// (The change.Encode function actually has its own fixup to just\n\t\t// quietly make this match change.Addr in the incorrect case, but we\n\t\t// intentionally panic here in order to catch incorrect callers where\n\t\t// the stack trace will hopefully be actually useful. The tolerance\n\t\t// at the next layer down is mainly to accommodate sloppy input in\n\t\t// older tests.)\n\t\tpanic(\"unpopulated ResourceInstanceChange.PrevRunAddr in writeChange\")\n\t}\n\n\tchanges.AppendResourceInstanceChange(change)\n\tif deposedKey == states.NotDeposed {\n\t\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] writeChange: recorded %s change for %s\", change.Action, NodeAbstractResourceInstance.refresh for %s (deposed object %s)\", absAddr, deposedKey)\n\t}\n\tprovider, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags.Append(err)\n\t}\n\t// If we have no state, we don't do any refreshing\n\tif state == nil {\n\t\tlog.Printf(\"[DEBUG] refresh: %s: no state, so not refreshing\", absAddr)\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\tschema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(n.Addr.Resource.ContainingResource())\n\tif schema == nil {\n\t\t// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(fmt.Errorf(\"provider does not support resource type %q\", n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type))\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\tmetaConfigVal, metaDiags := n.providerMetas(ctx)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(metaDiags)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// Call pre-refresh hook\n\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n\t\treturn h.PreRefresh(n.HookResourceIdentity(), deposedKey, state.Value)\n\t}))\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// Refresh!\n\tpriorVal := state.Value\n\n\t// Unmarked before sending to provider\n\tvar priorMarks []cty.PathValueMarks\n\tpriorVal, priorMarks = priorVal.UnmarkDeepWithPaths()\n\n\tvar resp providers.ReadResourceResponse\n\tif n.override != nil {\n\t\t// If we have an override set for this resource, we don't want to talk\n\t\t// to the provider so we'll just return whatever was in state.\n\t\tresp = providers.ReadResourceResponse{\n\t\t\tNewState: priorVal,\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\tresp = provider.ReadResource(providers.ReadResourceRequest{\n\t\t\tTypeName:     n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type,\n\t\t\tPriorState:   priorVal,\n\t\t\tPrivate:      state.Private,\n\t\t\tProviderMeta: metaConfigVal,\n\t\t\tClientCapabilities: providers.ClientCapabilities{\n\t\t\t\tDeferralAllowed: deferralAllowed,\n\t\t\t},\n\t\t})\n\n\t\t// If we don't support deferrals, but the provider reports a deferral and does not\n\t\t// emit any error level diagnostics, we should emit an error.\n\t\tif resp.Deferred != nil && !deferralAllowed && !resp.Diagnostics.HasErrors() {\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(deferring.UnexpectedProviderDeferralDiagnostic(n.Addr))\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif resp.Deferred != nil {\n\t\t\tdeferred = resp.Deferred\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\tif n.Config != nil {\n\t\tresp.Diagnostics = resp.Diagnostics.InConfigBody(n.Config.Config, n.Addr.String())\n\t}\n\n\tdiags = diags.Append(resp.Diagnostics)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\tif resp.NewState == cty.NilVal {\n\t\t// This ought not to happen in real cases since it's not possible to\n\t\t// send NilVal over the plugin RPC channel, but it can come up in\n\t\t// tests due to sloppy mocking.\n\t\tpanic(\"new state is cty.NilVal\")\n\t}\n\n\t// If we have deferred the refresh, we expect the new state not to be wholly known\n\t// and callers should be prepared to handle this.\n\tif !resp.NewState.IsWhollyKnown() && deferred == nil {\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid object\",\n\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q planned an invalid value for %s during refresh: %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.Provider, absAddr, \"The returned state contains unknown values\",\n\t\t\t),\n\t\t))\n\t}\n\n\tfor _, err := range resp.NewState.Type().TestConformance(schema.ImpliedType()) {\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid object\",\n\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q planned an invalid value for %s during refresh: %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.Provider, absAddr, tfdiags.FormatError(err),\n\t\t\t),\n\t\t))\n\t}\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn state, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\tnewState := objchange.NormalizeObjectFromLegacySDK(resp.NewState, schema)\n\tif !newState.RawEquals(resp.NewState) {\n\t\t// We had to fix up this object in some way, and we still need to\n\t\t// accept any changes for compatibility, so all we can do is log a\n\t\t// warning about the change.\n\t\tlog.Printf(\"[WARN] Provider %q produced an invalid new value containing null blocks for %q during refresh\\n\", n.ResolvedProvider.Provider, n.Addr)\n\t}\n\n\tret := state.DeepCopy()\n\tret.Value = newState\n\tret.Private = resp.Private\n\n\t// We have no way to exempt provider using the legacy SDK from this check,\n\t// so we can only log inconsistencies with the updated state values.\n\t// In most cases these are not errors anyway, and represent \"drift\" from\n\t// external changes which will be handled by the subsequent plan.\n\tif errs := objchange.AssertObjectCompatible(schema, priorVal, ret.Value); len(errs) > 0 {\n\t\tvar buf strings.Builder\n\t\tfmt.Fprintf(&buf, \"[WARN] Provider %q produced an unexpected new value for %s during refresh.\", n.ResolvedProvider.Provider.String(), absAddr)\n\t\tfor _, err := range errs {\n\t\t\tfmt.Fprintf(&buf, \"\\n      - %s\", tfdiags.FormatError(err))\n\t\t}\n\t\tlog.Print(buf.String())\n\t}\n\n\t// Call post-refresh hook\n\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n\t\treturn h.PostRefresh(n.HookResourceIdentity(), deposedKey, priorVal, ret.Value)\n\t}))\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn ret, deferred, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// Mark the value with any prior marks from the state, and the marks from\n\t// the schema. This ensures we capture any marks from the last\n\t// configuration, as well as any marks from the schema which were not in\n\t// the prior state. New marks may appear when the prior state was from an\n\t// import operation, or if the provider added new marks to the schema.\n\tret.Value = ret.Value.MarkWithPaths(priorMarks)\n\tif moreSensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(ret.Value, nil); len(moreSensitivePaths) != 0 {\n\t\tret.Value = marks.MarkPaths(ret.Value, marks.Sensitive, moreSensitivePaths)\n\t}\n\n\treturn ret, deferred, diags\n}\n\nfunc (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) plan(\n\tctx EvalContext,\n\tplannedChange *plans.ResourceInstanceChange,\n\tcurrentState *states.ResourceInstanceObject,\n\tcreateBeforeDestroy bool,\n\tforceReplace []addrs.AbsResourceInstance,\n) (*plans.ResourceInstanceChange, *states.ResourceInstanceObject, *providers.Deferred, instances.RepetitionData, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\n\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\n\tvar keyData instances.RepetitionData\n\tvar deferred *providers.Deferred\n\n\tresource := n.Addr.Resource.Resource\n\tdeferralAllowed := ctx.Deferrals().DeferralAllowed()\n\n\tprovider, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags.Append(err)\n\t}\n\n\tschema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(resource)\n\tif schema == nil {\n\t\t// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(fmt.Errorf(\"provider does not support resource type %q\", resource.Type))\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// If we're importing and generating config, generate it now.\n\tif n.Config == nil {\n\t\t// This shouldn't happen. A node that isn't generating config should\n\t\t// have embedded config, and the rest of Terraform should enforce this.\n\t\t// If, however, we didn't do things correctly the next line will panic,\n\t\t// so let's not do that and return an error message with more context.\n\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\"Resource has no configuration\",\n\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\"Terraform attempted to process a resource at %s that has no configuration. This is a bug in Terraform; please report it!\", n.Addr.String())))\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\tconfig := *n.Config\n\n\tcheckRuleSeverity := tfdiags.Error\n\tif n.preDestroyRefresh {\n\t\tcheckRuleSeverity = tfdiags.Warning\n\t}\n\n\tif plannedChange != nil {\n\t\t// If we already planned the action, we stick to that plan\n\t\tcreateBeforeDestroy = plannedChange.Action == plans.CreateThenDelete\n\t}\n\n\t// Evaluate the configuration\n\tforEach, _, _ := evaluateForEachExpression(n.Config.ForEach, ctx, false)\n\n\tkeyData = EvalDataForInstanceKey(n.ResourceInstanceAddr().Resource.Key, forEach)\n\n\tcheckDiags := evalCheckRules(\n\t\taddrs.ResourcePrecondition,\n\t\tn.Config.Preconditions,\n\t\tctx, n.Addr, keyData,\n\t\tcheckRuleSeverity,\n\t)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(checkDiags)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags // failed preconditions prevent further evaluation\n\t}\n\n\t// If we have a previous plan and the action was a noop, then the only\n\t// reason we're in this method was to evaluate the preconditions. There's\n\t// no need to re-plan this resource.\n\tif plannedChange != nil && plannedChange.Action == plans.NoOp {\n\t\treturn plannedChange, currentState.DeepCopy(), deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\torigConfigVal, _, configDiags := ctx.EvaluateBlock(config.Config, schema, nil, keyData)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(configDiags)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(\n\t\tvalidateResourceForbiddenEphemeralValues(ctx, origConfigVal, schema).InConfigBody(n.Config.Config, n.Addr.String()),\n\t)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\tmetaConfigVal, metaDiags := n.providerMetas(ctx)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(metaDiags)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\tvar priorVal cty.Value\n\tvar priorValTainted cty.Value\n\tvar priorPrivate []byte\n\tif currentState != nil {\n\t\tif currentState.Status != states.ObjectTainted {\n\t\t\tpriorVal = currentState.Value\n\t\t\tpriorPrivate = currentState.Private\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t// If the prior state is tainted then we'll proceed below like\n\t\t\t// we're creating an entirely new object, but then turn it into\n\t\t\t// a synthetic \"Replace\" change at the end, creating the same\n\t\t\t// result as if the provider had marked at least one argument\n\t\t\t// change as \"requires replacement\".\n\t\t\tpriorValTainted = currentState.Value\n\t\t\tpriorVal = cty.NullVal(schema.ImpliedType())\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\tpriorVal = cty.NullVal(schema.ImpliedType())\n\t}\n\n\tlog.Printf(\"[TRACE] Re-validating config for %q\", n.Addr)\n\t// Allow the provider to validate the final set of values.  The config was\n\t// statically validated early on, but there may have been unknown values\n\t// which the provider could not validate at the time.\n\t//\n\t// TODO: It would be more correct to validate the config after\n\t// ignore_changes has been applied, but the current implementation cannot\n\t// exclude computed-only attributes when given the `all` option.\n\n\t// we must unmark and use the original config, since the ignore_changes\n\t// handling below needs access to the marks.\n\tunmarkedConfigVal, _ := origConfigVal.UnmarkDeep()\n\tvalidateResp := provider.ValidateResourceConfig(\n\t\tproviders.ValidateResourceConfigRequest{\n\t\t\tTypeName: n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type,\n\t\t\tConfig:   unmarkedConfigVal,\n\t\t},\n\t)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(validateResp.Diagnostics.InConfigBody(config.Config, n.Addr.String()))\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// ignore_changes is meant to only apply to the configuration, so it must\n\t// be applied before we generate a plan. This ensures the config used for\n\t// the proposed value, the proposed value itself, and the config presented\n\t// to the provider in the PlanResourceChange request all agree on the\n\t// starting values.\n\t// Here we operate on the marked values, so as to revert any changes to the\n\t// marks as well as the value.\n\tconfigValIgnored, ignoreChangeDiags := n.processIgnoreChanges(priorVal, origConfigVal, schema)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(ignoreChangeDiags)\n\tif ignoreChangeDiags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// Create an unmarked version of our config val and our prior val.\n\t// Store the paths for the config val to re-mark after we've sent things\n\t// over the wire.\n\tunmarkedConfigVal, unmarkedPaths := configValIgnored.UnmarkDeepWithPaths()\n\tunmarkedPriorVal, _ := priorVal.UnmarkDeepWithPaths()\n\n\tproposedNewVal := objchange.ProposedNew(schema, unmarkedPriorVal, unmarkedConfigVal)\n\n\t// Call pre-diff hook\n\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\n\t\treturn h.PreDiff(n.HookResourceIdentity(), addrs.NotDeposed, priorVal, proposedNewVal)\n\t}))\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\tvar resp providers.PlanResourceChangeResponse\n\tif n.override != nil {\n\t\t// Then we have an override to apply for this change. But, overrides\n\t\t// only matter when we are creating a resource for the first time as we\n\t\t// only apply computed values.\n\t\tif priorVal.IsNull() {\n\t\t\t// Then we are actually creating something, so let's populate the\n\t\t\t// computed values from our override value.\n\t\t\toverride, overrideDiags := mocking.PlanComputedValuesForResource(proposedNewVal, schema)\n\t\t\tresp = providers.PlanResourceChangeResponse{\n\t\t\t\tPlannedState: override,\n\t\t\t\tDiagnostics:  overrideDiags,\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t// This is an update operation, and we don't actually have any\n\t\t\t// computed values that need to be applied.\n\t\t\tresp = providers.PlanResourceChangeResponse{\n\t\t\t\tPlannedState: proposedNewVal,\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\tresp = provider.PlanResourceChange(providers.PlanResourceChangeRequest{\n\t\t\tTypeName:         n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type,\n\t\t\tConfig:           unmarkedConfigVal,\n\t\t\tPriorState:       unmarkedPriorVal,\n\t\t\tProposedNewState: proposedNewVal,\n\t\t\tPriorPrivate:     priorPrivate,\n\t\t\tProviderMeta:     metaConfigVal,\n\t\t\tClientCapabilities: providers.ClientCapabilities{\n\t\t\t\tDeferralAllowed: deferralAllowed,\n\t\t\t},\n\t\t})\n\t\t// If we don't support deferrals, but the provider reports a deferral and does not\n\t\t// emit any error level diagnostics, we should emit an error.\n\t\tif resp.Deferred != nil && !deferralAllowed && !resp.Diagnostics.HasErrors() {\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(deferring.UnexpectedProviderDeferralDiagnostic(n.Addr))\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\tdiags = diags.Append(resp.Diagnostics.InConfigBody(config.Config, n.Addr.String()))\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\t// We mark this node as deferred at a later point when we know the complete change\n\tif resp.Deferred != nil {\n\t\tdeferred = resp.Deferred\n\t}\n\n\tplannedNewVal := resp.PlannedState\n\tplannedPrivate := resp.PlannedPrivate\n\n\t// These checks are only relevant if the provider is not deferring the\n\t// change.\n\tif deferred == nil {\n\t\tif plannedNewVal == cty.NilVal {\n\t\t\t// Should never happen. Since real-world providers return via RPC a nil\n\t\t\t// is always a bug in the client-side stub. This is more likely caused\n\t\t\t// by an incompletely-configured mock provider in tests, though.\n\t\t\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\"PlanResourceChange of %s produced nil value\", n.Addr))\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// We allow the planned new value to disagree with configuration _values_\n\t\t// here, since that allows the provider to do special logic like a\n\t\t// DiffSuppressFunc, but we still require that the provider produces\n\t\t// a value whose type conforms to the schema.\n\t\tfor _, err := range plannedNewVal.Type().TestConformance(schema.ImpliedType()) {\n\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid plan\",\n\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n\t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q planned an invalid value for %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n\t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.Provider, tfdiags.FormatErrorPrefixed(err, n.Addr.String()),\n\t\t\t\t),\n\t\t\t))\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif errs := objchange.AssertPlanValid(schema, unmarkedPriorVal, unmarkedConfigVal, plannedNewVal); len(errs) > 0 {\n\t\t\tif resp.LegacyTypeSystem {\n\t\t\t\t// The shimming of the old type system in the legacy SDK is not precise\n\t\t\t\t// enough to pass this consistency check, so we'll give it a pass here,\n\t\t\t\t// but we will generate a warning about it so that we are more likely\n\t\t\t\t// to notice in the logs if an inconsistency beyond the type system\n\t\t\t\t// leads to a downstream provider failure.\n\t\t\t\tvar buf strings.Builder\n\t\t\t\tfmt.Fprintf(&buf,\n\t\t\t\t\t\"[WARN] Provider %q produced an invalid plan for %s, but we are tolerating it because it is using the legacy plugin SDK.\\n    The following problems may be the cause of any confusing errors from downstream operations:\",\n\t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.Provider, n.Addr,\n\t\t\t\t)\n\t\t\t\tfor _, err := range errs {\n\t\t\t\t\tfmt.Fprintf(&buf, \"\\n      - %s\", tfdiags.FormatError(err))\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\tlog.Print(buf.String())\n\t\t\t} else {\n\t\t\t\tfor _, err := range errs {\n\t\t\t\t\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\n\t\t\t\t\t\ttfdiags.Error,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\"Provider produced invalid plan\",\n\t\t\t\t\t\tfmt.Sprintf(\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t\"Provider %q planned an invalid value for %s.\\n\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\",\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\tn.ResolvedProvider.Provider, tfdiags.FormatErrorPrefixed(err, n.Addr.String()),\n\t\t\t\t\t\t),\n\t\t\t\t\t))\n\t\t\t\t}\n\t\t\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tif resp.LegacyTypeSystem {\n\t\t// Because we allow legacy providers to depart from the contract and\n\t\t// return changes to non-computed values, the plan response may have\n\t\t// altered values that were already suppressed with ignore_changes.\n\t\t// A prime example of this is where providers attempt to obfuscate\n\t\t// config data by turning the config value into a hash and storing the\n\t\t// hash value in the state. There are enough cases of this in existing\n\t\t// providers that we must accommodate the behavior for now, so for\n\t\t// ignore_changes to work at all on these values, we will revert the\n\t\t// ignored values once more.\n\t\t// A nil schema is passed to processIgnoreChanges to indicate that we\n\t\t// don't want to fixup a config value according to the schema when\n\t\t// ignoring \"all\", rather we are reverting provider imposed changes.\n\t\tplannedNewVal, ignoreChangeDiags = n.processIgnoreChanges(unmarkedPriorVal, plannedNewVal, nil)\n\t\tdiags = diags.Append(ignoreChangeDiags)\n\t\tif ignoreChangeDiags.HasErrors() {\n\t\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\t// Add the marks back to the planned new value -- this must happen after\n\t// ignore changes have been processed. We add in the schema marks as well,\n\t// to ensure that provider defined private attributes are marked correctly\n\t// here.\n\tunmarkedPlannedNewVal := plannedNewVal\n\tplannedNewVal = plannedNewVal.MarkWithPaths(unmarkedPaths)\n\tif sensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(plannedNewVal, nil); len(sensitivePaths) != 0 {\n\t\tplannedNewVal = marks.MarkPaths(plannedNewVal, marks.Sensitive, sensitivePaths)\n\t}\n\n\treqRep, reqRepDiags := getRequiredReplaces(unmarkedPriorVal, unmarkedPlannedNewVal, resp.RequiresReplace, n.ResolvedProvider.Provider, n.Addr)\n\tdiags = diags.Append(reqRepDiags)\n\tif diags.HasErrors() {\n\t\treturn nil, nil, deferred, keyData, diags\n\t}\n\n\taction, actionReason := getAction(n.Addr, unmarkedPriorVal, unmarkedPlannedNewVal, createBeforeDestroy, forceReplace, reqRep)\n\n\tif action.IsReplace() {\n\t\t// In this strange situation we want to produce a change object that\n\t\t// shows our real prior object but has a _new_ object that is built\n\t\t// from a null prior object, since we're going to delete the one\n\t\t// that has all the computed values on it.\n\t\t//\n\t\t// Therefore we'll ask the provider to plan again here, giving it\n\t\t// a null object for the prior, and then we'll meld that with the\n\t\t// _actual_ prior state to produce a correctly-shaped replace change.\n\t\t// The resulting change should show any computed attributes changing\n\t\t// from known prior values to unknown values, unless the provider is\n\t\t// able to predict new values for any of these computed attributes.\n\t\tnullPriorVal := cty.NullVal(schema.ImpliedType())\n\n\t\t// Since there is no prior state to compare after replacement, we need\n\t\t// a new unmarked config from our original with no ignored values.\n\t\tunmarkedConfigVal := origConfigVal\n\t\tif origConfigVal.ContainsMarked() {\n\t\t\tunmarkedConfigVal, _ = origConfigVal.UnmarkDeep()\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t// create a new proposed value from the null state and the config\n\t\tproposedNewVal = objchange.ProposedNew(schema, nullPriorVal, unmarkedConfigVal)\n\n\t\tif n.override != nil {\n\t\t\t// In this case, we are always creating the resource so we don't\n\t\t\t// do any validation, and just call out to the mocking library.\n\t\t\toverride, overrideDiags := mocking.PlanComputedValuesForResource(proposedNewVal, schema)\n\t\t\tresp = providers.PlanResourceChangeResponse{\n\t\t\t\tPlannedState: override,\n\t\t\t\tDiagnostics:  overrideDiags,\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tresp = provider.PlanResourceChange(providers.PlanResourceChangeRequest{\n\t\t\t\tTypeName:         n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Type,\n\t\t\t\tConfig:           unmarkedConfigVal,\n\t\t\t\tPriorState:
